timeline.event: Born
Created the masterpieces 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana'
Created the masterpiece 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' (Old pond / a frog jumps in / sound of water)
Died (November 28, lunar October 12)

keyAchievements.title: Recognition of Haikai-no-renga style
keyAchievements.description: His works were recognized during his lifetime in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Haiku
keyAchievements.description: Today, he is considered a master of haiku, which was called hokku during his time.

keyAchievements.title: Internationally renowned poet
keyAchievements.description: His poetry is internationally renowned, and many of his poems in Japan are reproduced at monuments or historical sites.

keyAchievements.title: Fame across Japan
keyAchievements.description: He entered the world of poetry at a young age, and by integrating himself into the intellectual landscape of Edo, he became famous throughout Japan.

keyAchievements.title: Inspiration through travel
keyAchievements.description: He wandered throughout Japan, including the north, east, and west, to gain inspiration for his works.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry based on experiences of nature
keyAchievements.description: His poetry was influenced by his direct experiences of the world around him, condensing emotions about landscapes into simple elements.

faq.question: Who was Matsuo Basho?
faq.answer: Matsuo Basho, born in 1644 and died on November 28, 1694 (lunar October 12), was a poet of the early Edo period. His childhood name was Kinsaku, and he was also known as Matsuo Chuemon Munefusa.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's major achievements?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, his works were recognized in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style, and today he is considered a master of haiku. His poetry is internationally renowned and reproduced at monuments and historical sites across Japan.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's representative works?
faq.answer: Representative works include 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana' from 1685, and 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' from 1686.

faq.question: How did Matsuo Basho conduct his literary activities?
faq.answer: While making a living as a teacher, he distanced himself from the social and urban life where literary circles were based, wandering throughout Japan to gain inspiration. His poetry was influenced by direct experiences of the surrounding world, condensing his emotions about landscapes.

faq.question: Which of his works did Matsuo Basho consider most important?
faq.answer: He believed his best work lay in participating in and leading renku, and considered the connection with haikai verses more important than hokku.

timeline.event: Born as the second child of Edward John Wollstonecraft and Elizabeth Dickson in Spitalfields, London
Became independent and worked as a companion to Sarah Dawson in Bath, Somerset
Returned home to care for her mother on her deathbed
Persuaded her sister Eliza, who was suffering from postpartum depression, to emancipate herself from her household
Left her school and followed her friend Fanny Blood to Lisbon to nurse her
Published 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters'; decided to become an author
Published her first novel 'Mary: A Fiction'; published her only children's book 'Original Stories from Real Life'
Wrote 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' in response to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France'
Wrote her most famous and influential work 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'; left for Paris in December and returned a month before Louis XVI's execution
France declared war on Britain
The Jacobin-led Committee of Public Safety seized power in France
Fell in love with American adventurer Gilbert Imlay; Imlay falsely registered Wollstonecraft as his wife to obtain American citizenship
Gave birth to her first daughter, Fanny Imlay
The Jacobins fell from power
'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution', written in Le Havre, Northern France, was published in London
Left France
Returned to London looking for Imlay, but Imlay refused to reconcile
Attempted suicide with laudanum but was saved by Imlay
Letters written to Imlay during her Scandinavian trip were compiled and published as 'Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark'
Moved to Somers Town, northwest London, with William Godwin
Gave birth to her second daughter, Mary
Died of puerperal fever (at age 38)
Her unfinished work 'Maria' was published posthumously
Her husband William Godwin published 'Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' (1792)
keyAchievements.description: The first feminist philosophical work arguing that women are not naturally inferior to men, but appear to be only because they lack education, advocating for women's right to education and equal fundamental rights.

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' (1790)
keyAchievements.description: A rebuttal to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France', and the first political and feminist writing to attack aristocracy, defend republicanism, and criticize Burke's gender-biased language.

keyAchievements.title: 'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution' (1794)
keyAchievements.description: A book that outlined the early history of the French Revolution, countered the counter-revolutionary atmosphere in Britain, and argued that the revolution was the result of social, economic, and political crises.

keyAchievements.title: 'Letters Written in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark' (1796)
keyAchievements.description: A travelogue containing reflections on the society and people of Scandinavia, questions of identity, and her relationship with Imlay, exploring the value of subjective experience and imagination, and advocating for women's liberation and education.

keyAchievements.title: Educational writings
keyAchievements.description: Argued through 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters' (1787) and children's literature 'Original Stories from Real Life' (1788) that children should be taught to think and receive an education that fosters middle-class ethos.

faq.question: What is Mary Wollstonecraft's most famous book?
faq.answer: Her most famous book is 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman', published in 1792.

faq.question: What did Mary Wollstonecraft argue about women's education?
faq.answer: She argued that women are not inherently inferior to men, but only appear to be due to a lack of education, and therefore should receive quality education.

faq.question: Are any of Mary Wollstonecraft's daughters famous?
faq.answer: Yes, her second daughter, Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin, later married Percy Bysshe Shelley and became Mary Shelley, the author of the novel 'Frankenstein'.

faq.question: With which philosophers did Mary Wollstonecraft associate?
faq.answer: She associated with radical writer Thomas Paine, philosopher William Godwin, and others, and notably married William Godwin.

faq.question: What was Mary Wollstonecraft's stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She condemned the Jacobin regime and the Reign of Terror, but maintained the position that the revolution was a great achievement.

timeline.event: Born to Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II bestowed the title of King on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
Succeeded to the throne after the death of his father Pepin the Short, taking over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman I.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Destroyed and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the Basques at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during an expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome, had his two sons Pepin and Louis consecrated, and bestowed upon them the crowns of Lombardy and Aquitaine respectively.
His wife Hildegard and mother Bertrada of Laon passed away successively, and he remarried Fastrada.
Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria.
Soundly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them beyond the borders.
Built a palace and royal church in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it the de facto capital.
Pope Leo III, attacked by opponents, fled to Charlemagne's court to seek assistance.
Was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III in St. Peter's Basilica.
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
The Avar Khagan Zodan visited Aachen, swore fealty to Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chiefs, including Viseslav, voluntarily surrendered.
Eastern Roman Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son Louis I the Pious as the legitimate successor at the court in Aachen and crowned him co-emperor.
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Pope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne as a saint at the request of Frederick I.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by conquering most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Crowned Emperor of the Romans
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800, politically and religiously unifying most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Carolingian Renaissance
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing arts, religion, and culture through the church during his reign.

keyAchievements.title: Codification of laws and centralization
keyAchievements.description: Codified the laws of various tribes to rule over vast territories and enabled centralized rule by dispatching officials from the center.

keyAchievements.title: Spread and integration of Christianity
keyAchievements.description: Sought ideological unity by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of cultural and educational institutions
keyAchievements.description: Led a revival of learning and arts by building a palace and royal church in Aachen, and founding a royal library and palace school.

faq.question: When was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: There are theories that Charlemagne was born on April 2 of 740 or 742, or in 747.

faq.question: Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the foundation for the current identity of Europe.

faq.question: When did Charlemagne become Emperor of the Romans?
faq.answer: Charlemagne received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800.

faq.question: How tall was Charlemagne?
faq.answer: According to Einhard's records and the results of a tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne was about 190 to 191 cm tall.

faq.question: What languages did Charlemagne speak?
faq.answer: In addition to his mother tongue, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak some Greek.

faq.question: Where was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.

timeline.event: Giuseppe Maria Garibaldi, an Italian general and patriot, is born.
Certified as a merchant navy captain.
Met Giovanni Battista Cuneo, an Italian political immigrant, in Taganrog, Russia, and joined the Young Italy movement.
Sentenced to death after participating in the Piedmont rebellion and fled to Marseille.
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil and aided the independence of the Riograndense Republic.
Anita Ribeiro joined Garibaldi's ship 'Rio Pardo'.
First son Menotti is born.
Moved to Montevideo and worked as a trader and teacher.
Married Anita in Montevideo; commanded the Uruguayan fleet and raised the 'Italian Legion'.
Second daughter Rosita is born; formed the Italian Legion in Montevideo.
Third daughter Teresita is born.
Achieved victory at the Battles of Cerro and San Antonio.
Returned to his homeland Italy upon hearing news of the revolution breaking out in Palermo.
Promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army; fled to northern Italy after the fall of the Roman Republic; his wife Anita died during the retreat.
Became a resident of New York.
Sailed to the mouth of the River Tyne in North East England as the master of the sailing ship Commonwealth.
Returned to Italy.
Outbreak of the Second Italian War of Independence; led the 'Hunters of the Alps' and captured Varese and Como.
Landed in Marsala, Sicily leading a thousand volunteers.
Defeated the Bourbon French garrison at Calatafimi.
Launched a siege on Palermo.
Entered the capital of Naples.
The volunteers achieved success at the Battle of the Volturno.
Met and shook hands with Victor Emmanuel II in Teano.
The unified Kingdom of Italy was established.
Left Genoa, landed in Palermo, and gathered volunteers under the slogan 'Rome or Death'.
Landed at Melito and marched into the Calabrian mountains.
Met regular forces at the Aspromonte mountains and was taken prisoner after a battle.
With the backing of the Italian government, recruited the 'Hunters of the Alps', led them into Trentino, and defeated the Austrian army at Bezzecca.
Marched on Rome again but was stopped by the Papal army supported by French reinforcements.
The Franco-Prussian War broke out.
Died on the island of Caprera.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Italian unification
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of Two Worlds
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Hero of Two Worlds' for his military achievements across South America and Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Support for South American independence
keyAchievements.description: Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

keyAchievements.title: Italian military command
keyAchievements.description: Appointed general by the provisional government of Milan and promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Hunters of the Alps
keyAchievements.description: Led the 'Hunters of the Alps' in the Second Italian War of Independence, capturing Varese and Como.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Expedition of the Thousand
keyAchievements.description: Led an expedition of a thousand volunteers to successfully annex Sicily, Southern Italy, Marche, and Umbria to the Kingdom of Sardinia.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Franco-Prussian War
keyAchievements.description: Served as commander of the Army of the Vosges during the Franco-Prussian War.

keyAchievements.title: International symbol
keyAchievements.description: Became an international symbol for realizing the ideals of national independence and republicanism.

keyAchievements.title: Efforts to limit papal power
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to anti-clericalism among Latin liberals and did much to limit the temporal power of the Pope.

faq.question: Who was Giuseppe Garibaldi?
faq.answer: An Italian general and patriot who contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

faq.question: Why is Garibaldi called the 'Hero of Two Worlds'?
faq.answer: Because his military achievements span across South America and Europe.

faq.question: What were Garibaldi's political beliefs?
faq.answer: He was a republican who dedicated himself to unifying Italy under a democratic republican government.

faq.question: What is Garibaldi's iconic attire?
faq.answer: It is related to the red shirts worn by his volunteers instead of uniforms.

faq.question: What activities did Garibaldi undertake in South America?
faq.answer: He joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to aid the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

faq.question: Did Garibaldi have a chance to participate in the American Civil War?
faq.answer: He was offered a position as a major general in the Union Army by President Abraham Lincoln, but the offer fell through when he proposed the abolition of slavery and the position of Commander-in-Chief as conditions.

faq.question: Where is Garibaldi's hometown?
faq.answer: He was born in Nice (Nizza), currently in France, while it was under French rule in 1792.

faq.question: With whom did Garibaldi have a conflict after Italian unification?
faq.answer: He deeply disliked the Sardinian Prime Minister Camillo di Cavour, owing to personal resentment over Cavour's pragmatism and the selling of Nice to the French.

timeline.event: Zenobia, a Palmyrene noblewoman, married King Odaenathus
Odaenathus was assassinated, and Zenobia became the regent of the Palmyrene Empire in place of her young son Vaballathus
Zenobia successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt
Declared Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and herself as Empress of Palmyra
Defeated by Roman Emperor Aurelian and taken to Rome as a captive, bringing the Palmyrene Empire to ruin
자브다스 장군이 이끄는 팔미라 제국 군대가 이집트와 시리아를 정복함.
팔미라 제국 군대가 이집트 총독을 살해함.
팔미라 제국이 아나톨리아와 팔레스타인을 정복하며 영토를 확장함.
로마 제국의 아우렐리아누스 황제가 팔미라 제국 정복을 결심하고 군대를 이끌고 침공함.
로마 제국이 팔미라 제국을 정복함.
로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에서 포로로 끌려옴.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Palmyrene Empire
keyAchievements.description: After her husband's death, she ruled the Palmyrene Empire as regent and expanded its territory by conquering Roman Egypt.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of an independent state
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to establish an independent state from the Roman Empire by capturing large areas of Roman territory, including the Levant and Asia Minor.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Imperial titles
keyAchievements.description: Declared her son Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and elevated herself to Empress of Palmyra.

faq.question: Who was Zenobia?
faq.answer: A noblewoman and ruler of the Palmyrene Empire, who acted as regent after her husband Odaenathus was assassinated, bringing the empire to its peak before clashing with the Roman Empire and eventually leading to its downfall.

faq.question: What territories did Zenobia conquer?
faq.answer: She successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt, the Levant, and Asia Minor.

faq.question: How did Zenobia's rule come to an end?
faq.answer: She was defeated by the newly ascended Roman Emperor Aurelian, taken to Rome as a captive, and the Palmyrene Empire fell.

faq.question: 제노비아는 어떻게 팔미라 제국의 통치자가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 267년 오데나투스 황제 암살 후 아들 바발라투스를 후계자로 임명하며 팔미라 제국의 공동 통치자가 되었습니다.

faq.question: 제노비아의 최후는 어떻게 되었나요?
faq.answer: 274년 로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에 포로로 끌려왔으나, 그 이후의 정확한 생사는 불명확합니다.
